title: Art journal gesso ground
subtitle: Applying and texturing gesso on journal pages to create a stable mixed-media surface
excerpt: Plain journal paper buckles and bleeds when wet media is applied directly. A layer of gesso seals the surface, adds tooth for paint and pencil, and prevents bleed-through to the reverse page. The application method and the number of coats change the texture: smooth gesso suits fine detail; coarse or layered gesso suits impasto and collage.
sourceNotes: Gesso as a ground for mixed-media journalling is a contemporary craft practice documented widely from the early 2000s onward. No pre-1928 primary source covers the art-journal application. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A layer of acrylic gesso applied to a journal page to seal the surface, prevent bleed-through, and provide tooth for wet and dry media.
The micro-texture of a surface that allows dry media such as pencil, pastel, and coloured pencil to grip rather than slide off.

content.text: Acrylic gesso is a polymer-bound white primer with calcium carbonate filler. When applied to journal paper it creates a
that accepts ink, watercolour, acrylic, oil pastel, and collage without the page buckling or tearing. The
of the dried gesso layer determines which dry media can be used over it.
White acrylic gesso, undiluted from the container. Most brands are sold ready to use; diluting with water reduces the sealing effect on thin paper.
A wide flat brush, 25 mm or wider, for even coverage. A palette knife for textured application.
Insert a sheet of scrap card behind the page being gessoed to prevent bleed-through to the facing page while the gesso is wet.
Load the flat brush with gesso and apply in parallel strokes from top to bottom. Work quickly; gesso dries fast and re-brushing over tacky gesso lifts the surface.
Allow to dry fully (15 to 20 minutes at room temperature).
Apply a second coat at 90° to the first. Two thin coats give better coverage and less buckling than one thick coat.
Apply gesso thickly with the palette knife, working in uneven strokes to build ridges and peaks.
Press a crumpled piece of cling film or a coarse cloth into the wet gesso and peel away to leave an irregular texture.
Dry thoroughly before applying any further media. Thick gesso layers can take 30 to 60 minutes to dry through.

attrs.symptom: Page buckles badly after gesso application
attrs.cause: Gesso applied too wet or too thickly on lightweight paper.

content.text: Place the closed journal under a heavy weight for 30 minutes after the gesso dries. For future pages, apply thinner coats or add a small amount of acrylic medium to reduce shrinkage.
